The barrier to change is rarely a lack of ideas. It is the absence of a structured process and the psychological safety to pursue them. An innovation mindset is the internal conviction that you are not just an employee within a healthcare system, but a designer of it — empowered to bridge the gap between current limitations and future possibilities. Today's journey begins with a fundamental reframe: from Clinical Reactor to Systemic Innovator. National Academy of Medicine (2019): Taking Action Against Clinician Burnout.

text_blocks: Welcome & The Modern Clinician's Dilemma
TEJAL NAIK, M.D. — HOSPITALIST & ENTREPRENEUR-IN-RESIDENCE

You were trained to follow evidence-based pathways — not to create them. The barrier to change is rarely a lack of ideas. It is the absence of a structured process and the psychological safety to pursue them.

An innovation mindset is the internal conviction that you are not just an employee within a healthcare system, but a designer of it — empowered to bridge the gap between current limitations and future possibilities.
Today's journey begins with a fundamental reframe: from Clinical Reactor to Systemic Innovator. National Academy of Medicine (2019): Taking Action Against Clinician Burnout.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The Innovator's "Hero's Journey" This certificate unlocks a skill set you already possess as clinicians — reframed as a personal and professional quest. Three waypoints mark your path from insight to lasting impact. Executive One-Pager & Playbook Sparking Innovation: From Insight to Opportunity Building the Solution: Design, Plan, and Pitch Driving Impact: From Project to Lasting Change By journey's end, you will hold a tangible innovation playbook and the foundation for an executive one-pager — real deliverables that demonstrate systemic leadership. You are here: Waypoint 1.

text_blocks: The Innovator's "Hero's Journey"
This certificate unlocks a skill set you already possess as clinicians — reframed as a personal and professional quest. Three waypoints mark your path from insight to lasting impact.
Executive One-Pager & Playbook
Sparking Innovation: From Insight to Opportunity
Building the Solution: Design, Plan, and Pitch
Driving Impact: From Project to Lasting Change
By journey's end, you will hold a tangible innovation playbook and the foundation for an executive one-pager — real deliverables that demonstrate systemic leadership. You are here: Waypoint 1.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The Lineage of Physician Innovators Physicians have always been architects of change. These five exemplars demonstrate that innovation in healthcare spans care delivery, informatics, system redesign, process science, and digital health. Emergency Physicians CARE-DELIVERY REDESIGN During the pandemic, ED physicians rapidly deployed tele-triage to evaluate patients remotely, cutting exposure and preserving capacity. (ACEP, May 2020) Clinical Informatics & Abridge INFORMATICS Physician-led EMR adoption and Abridge AI conversation summarization improved documentation efficiency, reduced burnout, and enhanced patient communication. Glenn Steele — Geisinger SYSTEM REDESIGN ProvenCare (warranty-based surgical care) and ProvenHealth Navigator (advanced medical home) backed by data infrastructure and dedicated innovation teams. (PMC4106555) Dr. Atul Gawande PROCESS INNOVATION Adapted aviation's Checklist Manifesto for the OR — a process innovation, not a drug or device, that measurably reduced surgical mortality. (PMC3960713) Dr. Michelle Longmire — Medable DIGITAL HEALTH A dermatologist who built a platform for decentralized clinical trials, removing the brick-and-mortar bottleneck to make research faster and more equitable.

text_blocks: The Lineage of Physician Innovators

Physicians have always been architects of change. These five exemplars demonstrate that innovation in healthcare spans care delivery, informatics, system redesign, process science, and digital health.
Emergency Physicians CARE-DELIVERY REDESIGN During the pandemic, ED physicians rapidly deployed tele-triage to evaluate patients remotely, cutting exposure and preserving capacity. (ACEP, May 2020)
Clinical Informatics & Abridge INFORMATICS Physician-led EMR adoption and Abridge AI conversation summarization improved documentation efficiency, reduced burnout, and enhanced patient communication.

Glenn Steele — Geisinger SYSTEM REDESIGN ProvenCare (warranty-based surgical care) and ProvenHealth Navigator (advanced medical home) backed by data infrastructure and dedicated innovation teams. (PMC4106555)
Dr. Atul Gawande PROCESS INNOVATION Adapted aviation's Checklist Manifesto for the OR — a process innovation, not a drug or device, that measurably reduced surgical mortality. (PMC3960713)

Dr. Michelle Longmire — Medable DIGITAL HEALTH A dermatologist who built a platform for decentralized clinical trials, removing the brick-and-mortar bottleneck to make research faster and more equitable.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: Expectations & Mental Frameworks Two foundational concepts underpin everything in this course. Internalizing them is not optional — they are the psychological infrastructure that makes innovation possible. MENTAL STRENGTH PERSONAL GROWTH Growth Mindset — Carol Dweck The belief that abilities and intelligence are developed through effort, effective strategies, and learning from mistakes — not fixed traits. Your capacity to innovate is a muscle, not a ceiling. Failure is data, not verdict. Psychological Safety — Amy Edmondson A shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — speaking up, admitting mistakes, offering dissenting ideas — without fear of embarrassment or retaliation. Safety is the prerequisite, not the reward. Course contract: We collectively commit to psychological safety in all discussion boards and peer reviews. Share half-baked ideas. Critique systems, not people. Expose flaws without fear.

text_blocks: Expectations & Mental Frameworks
Two foundational concepts underpin everything in this course. Internalizing them is not optional — they are the psychological infrastructure that makes innovation possible.
MENTAL STRENGTH PERSONAL GROWTH
Growth Mindset — Carol Dweck

The belief that abilities and intelligence are developed through effort, effective strategies, and learning from mistakes — not fixed traits. Your capacity to innovate is a muscle, not a ceiling. Failure is data, not verdict.
Psychological Safety — Amy Edmondson

A shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — speaking up, admitting mistakes, offering dissenting ideas — without fear of embarrassment or retaliation. Safety is the prerequisite, not the reward.
Course contract: We collectively commit to psychological safety in all discussion boards and peer reviews. Share half-baked ideas. Critique systems, not people. Expose flaws without fear.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The Innovation Lexicon A shared vocabulary is the foundation of shared understanding. These eight definitions form the conceptual bedrock of this course — return to them often. Term Definition Innovation Creating new value that is adopted by the market or system — not just inventing a new gadget. Intrapreneurship Acting like an entrepreneur but driving innovation from within an established organization (e.g., a hospital system) while navigating legacy constraints. Systemic Friction Chronic, recurring operational or clinical pain points that drain time, money, and morale across the system. Root Cause vs. Symptom The symptom is the visible pain (e.g., high nursing turnover); the root cause is the invisible system failure creating it (e.g., a broken EHR triage workflow). Functional Flexibility The ability to adapt your skills and borrow concepts from outside your specialty to solve ambiguous, complex problems. First Principles Thinking Breaking a complex systemic problem into its most basic foundational truths and building up from there — not reasoning by analogy or "how we've always done it." Psychological Safety The shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — report errors, ask questions, propose unconventional ideas without fear of humiliation or retaliation. Growth Mindset The belief that clinical and leadership capabilities are built through dedication and iteration — shifting failure response from "I am not equipped" to "What did this failure teach us?"
perception_artifacts.layout_description: A large dark-blue title sits at the upper left with a short explanatory sentence below it. Most of the slide is occupied by a two-column lexicon table with a dark navy header and eight definition rows.

reading_path_rationale.image_role: No separate image element is present; the table is the primary textual content rather than an image to narrate.
reading_path_rationale.macro_layout: Title, intro sentence, and the large lexicon table stack vertically in a top-down text layout.
reading_path_rationale.narration_cadence: The slide contains many dense definitions requiring a slower scaffolded walkthrough.
reading_path_rationale.text_substructure: The eight term-definition rows are coordinate lexicon entries and can be referenced as peer items, not a sequence.

text_blocks: The Innovation Lexicon
A shared vocabulary is the foundation of shared understanding. These eight definitions form the conceptual bedrock of this course — return to them often.
Term Definition
Innovation Creating new value that is adopted by the market or system — not just inventing a new gadget.
Intrapreneurship Acting like an entrepreneur but driving innovation from within an established organization (e.g., a hospital system) while navigating legacy constraints.
Systemic Friction Chronic, recurring operational or clinical pain points that drain time, money, and morale across the system.
Root Cause vs. Symptom The symptom is the visible pain (e.g., high nursing turnover); the root cause is the invisible system failure creating it (e.g., a broken EHR triage workflow).
Functional Flexibility The ability to adapt your skills and borrow concepts from outside your specialty to solve ambiguous, complex problems.
First Principles Thinking Breaking a complex systemic problem into its most basic foundational truths and building up from there — not reasoning by analogy or "how we've always done it."
Psychological Safety The shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — report errors, ask questions, propose unconventional ideas without fear of humiliation or retaliation.

Growth Mindset The belief that clinical and leadership capabilities are built through dedication and iteration — shifting failure response from "I am not equipped" to "What did this failure teach us?"

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The Tightrope & The Net You cannot expect clinical staff to take the interpersonal risks that innovation requires if there is no safety net for failure. Safety precedes innovation. No Safety Net: Compliance & Fear When teams fear judgment or retaliation, they default to rigid compliance. Every misstep feels catastrophic. Innovation dies before it can be spoken aloud. Psychological Safety: Innovation & Risk-Taking When a net exists, clinicians attempt bold moves — proposing new workflows, flagging failures early, and piloting unconventional solutions. The net is the precondition for flight.

text_blocks: The Tightrope & The Net
You cannot expect clinical staff to take the interpersonal risks that innovation requires if there is no safety net for failure. Safety precedes innovation.
No Safety Net: Compliance & Fear
When teams fear judgment or retaliation, they default to rigid compliance. Every misstep feels catastrophic. Innovation dies before it can be spoken aloud.
Psychological Safety: Innovation & Risk-Taking
When a net exists, clinicians attempt bold moves — proposing new workflows, flagging failures early, and piloting unconventional solutions. The net is the precondition for flight.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The "Wicked" World of Innovation You have been rewarded for hyper-specialization — mastering Kind learning environments where patterns repeat and rules are clear. But healthcare's systemic problems are Wicked environments. Solving them demands functional flexibility and interdisciplinary range. Featured: David Epstein "Why the Most Experienced People Fail First" — on why generalists who borrow tools across domains triumph in complex systems. Watch on YouTube Post-video takeaway: To be an effective healthcare intrapreneur, you must have "Range" — the humility to set aside the specific tools that made you a great clinician and adopt the broad, interdisciplinary tools needed to redesign a broken system. Kind Environments (Clinical) Patterns repeat reliably Rules are clear and stable Feedback is immediate Specialization is rewarded Wicked Environments (Innovation) Patterns are ambiguous Rules shift with context Feedback is delayed or absent Range and flexibility win

text_blocks: The "Wicked" World of Innovation

You have been rewarded for hyper-specialization — mastering Kind learning environments where patterns repeat and rules are clear. But healthcare's systemic problems are Wicked environments. Solving them demands functional flexibility and interdisciplinary range.
Featured: David Epstein
"Why the Most Experienced People Fail First" — on why generalists who borrow tools across domains triumph in complex systems.
Watch on YouTube

Post-video takeaway: To be an effective healthcare intrapreneur, you must have "Range" — the humility to set aside the specific tools that made you a great clinician and adopt the broad, interdisciplinary tools needed to redesign a broken system.
Kind Environments (Clinical) • Patterns repeat reliably • Rules are clear and stable • Feedback is immediate • Specialization is rewarded
Wicked Environments (Innovation) • Patterns are ambiguous • Rules shift with context • Feedback is delayed or absent • Range and flexibility win

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: The Track vs. The Jungle Clinical training prepares you to excel in a predictable, well-marked environment. System innovation requires an entirely different toolkit — one built for ambiguity, uncharted territory, and improvisation under pressure. Kind Learning Environment (Clinical Specialization) Clear lanes. Defined rules. Measurable outcomes. Repetition builds mastery. The expert runner knows every hurdle. Wicked Learning Environment (Functional Flexibility) No map. No prescribed path. Success demands adaptability, a diverse toolkit, and the courage to move forward without a clear trail.

perception_artifacts.extracted_text: Part 1 Summary Before moving to Part 2 — Building the Solution — lock in the four pillars of Part 1. These are the lens through which every future module is interpreted. The Gap Traditional clinical training creates expert practitioners — but not necessarily system innovators. The skills that make a great clinician are not identical to those that redesign broken systems. The Mission Move from being an employee within the system to an innovation leader shaping the system — a designer who bridges the gap between current limitations and future possibilities. The Journey A three-waypoint progression: identifying opportunities → designing and pitching solutions → driving lasting, measurable impact. Your deliverable: a tangible playbook and executive one-pager. The Mindset Success requires agility, first principles thinking, psychological safety, and the willingness to embrace intelligent failure — treating every setback as signal, not verdict. Up Next — Part 2: Building the Solution: Design, Plan, and Pitch. Bring your identified friction points and unmet needs.
perception_artifacts.layout_description: A large title sits at the top left, followed by a two-line introductory sentence. Four bordered summary cards are arranged in a two-by-two grid, and a wide pale green callout bar spans the bottom.

reading_path_rationale.callout_intent: The bottom callout gives a next-step instruction to bring friction points and unmet needs.
reading_path_rationale.image_role: Only image-like element is a small icon marking the bottom callout, so it is pointer/iconographic.
reading_path_rationale.macro_layout: Four bordered summary cards form a two-by-two peer grid with a bottom callout bar.
reading_path_rationale.narration_cadence: Multiple cards contain substantial explanatory copy plus intro and next-step text.
reading_path_rationale.text_substructure: The four pillars are coordinate summary boxes, not a required transform sequence.

text_blocks: Part 1 Summary
Before moving to Part 2 — Building the Solution — lock in the four pillars of Part 1. These are the lens through which every future module is interpreted.

The Gap
Traditional clinical training creates expert practitioners — but not necessarily system innovators. The skills that make a great clinician are not identical to those that redesign broken systems.

The Mission
Move from being an employee within the system to an innovation leader shaping the system — a designer who bridges the gap between current limitations and future possibilities.

The Journey
A three-waypoint progression: identifying opportunities → designing and pitching solutions → driving lasting, measurable impact. Your deliverable: a tangible playbook and executive one-pager.

The Mindset
Success requires agility, first principles thinking, psychological safety, and the willingness to embrace intelligent failure — treating every setback as signal, not verdict.
Up Next — Part 2: Building the Solution: Design, Plan, and Pitch. Bring your identified friction points and unmet needs.
